schools.summary: The mountain/desert teaches against ostentation; teacher and hearer must stand on high spiritual ground (Isa 40:9).
The mount = greater commandments given in love; sitting = teaching authority / the Incarnation (Rabanus).
'The mountain' (article) near Capernaum, the 'Sinai of the NT'; harmonizes prayer, choosing the Twelve, and teaching.
One sermon, not two: Matthew and Luke each collect the leading points of Christ's doctrine.
The 'disciples' are not the Twelve only but all who desired to learn.

debates.question: Is this the same sermon as Luke 6 (mount vs plain)?

sides.position: the same discourse, a digest of Christ's teaching
perhaps repeated on different occasions
a Galilean hill near Capernaum
Mount Tabor

outliers.note: Mystically reads Christ's sitting down as his Incarnation, by which mankind could come to him.

schools.summary: Inchoative imperfect 'began to teach'; seated rabbinic posture; spoke loud for the great throng (Lk 6:17).
'Opened his mouth' is a Hebrew pleonasm marking a weighty, solemn discourse.
To make men happy was Christ's great business; he pronounces eight blessings, directing the instinct for happiness to its true object.
Leo: 'in spirit' shows humility, not mere want, wins the kingdom; the curse-ending OT meets the blessing-opening gospel (Henry).
Luke's plain 'poor' vs Matthew's 'poor in spirit'; the humble who own they have nothing of their own; makarios = truly happy.
The afflicted humbled and emptied of confidence in the flesh, submitting wholly to God; Luke and Matthew mean the same.
The unfeignedly penitent, deeply sensible of sinfulness and helplessness.

debates.question: Is the blessing on outward poverty (Luke) or humility (Matthew)?

sides.position: inward humility / poverty of spirit
Luke's wording points to outward poverty as a less perilous state

schools.summary: Mourning is godly sorrow for sin (one's own and others'), comforted by the Paraclete with eternal joys; not worldly grief (Leo).
Two senses possible, but chiefly sorrow over sin (2 Cor 7:10); the gospel alone truly comforts affliction (Isa 61).
Mourning, pursued wholly, crowds out other passions; God's mercies exceed our troubles.
Sorrow prepares for eternal joy and drives us to God; tribulation works patience and hope (Rom 5).

sides.position: sorrow for sin (one's own and others')
both worldly affliction and sorrow for sin

schools.summary: The meek resist not wrongs but overcome evil with good; 'the earth' = the perpetual inheritance or the glorified flesh of the saints (Hilary, Leo).
Meekness = resentment under control, not cowardice (cf. Jn 18:23; Acts 16:37); the gentleness of strength (Mt 11:29); echo of Dan 7:27.
Chrysostom keeps it partly present and literal (Ps 37:11): the meek hold their own in security.
A paradox: the violent seem to win the earth, yet Christ promises it to the gentle; the new earth of righteousness (Wesley).

debates.question: What is 'the earth' the meek inherit?

sides.position: present security / one's own possessed in peace (Ps 37:11)
the eternal/renewed inheritance or the glorified body

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, citing Isaiah 9 rather than commenting on meekness.

schools.summary: Desire must amount to hunger; the food is to do the Father's will and be filled with the Lord himself (Augustine, Leo; Ps 34:8).
Hunger/thirst = the keenest desire, fit emblem of the sense of sin and want (Ps 42); 'filled' (chortazō) = fully fed.
Either general righteousness or the virtue opposed to covetousness, which truly makes rich.
A figure for suffering want and being defrauded of one's right; the class stands for all the afflicted godly.
Justice and mercy united; mercy beyond alms, bearing a brother's burden; God shows mercy only to the merciful (Hilary, Leo).
Mercy = misericordia, 'pain of heart,' covering pardon of injuries and almsgiving; an evidence of piety done to glorify God (Mt 25:34-40).
The reward only seems an equal return; divine mercy far exceeds human.
A paradox: the world counts the carefree happy, but Christ blesses those who share others' afflictions.
God is seen by the heart, not the bodily eye; the pure/single heart is cleansed by faith (Acts 15:9); now in part, then face to face (1 Cor 13:12).
Inward purity against Pharisaic ceremonial cleanness; purity in its widest sense (not hate or greed only); seeing God a term of high favour (Rev 22:4).
The pure are conscious of no evil thoughts and keep the holiness without which none sees God (Heb 12:14).
Purity = sincerity against the world's prized craftiness; speaking nothing not felt in the heart.
Peace begins within (subduing carnal desire) then reconciles others; the reward of adoption (Hilary); set seventh = sabbath rest.
Not merely peaceable but actively making peace where it is not; follows purity of heart; the perfect peacemaker is the Son of God (Eph 2:14).
Reconciling the estranged was the chief work of the Only-begotten, so peacemakers are his brethren.
Reconcilers endure reproaches on all sides, so must look to God's judgment; the peace meant agrees with God's will (Leo).

outliers.note: Reads the peacemaker also as one who reconciles the apparent conflicts of Scripture (Law with Prophets, Old with New).

schools.summary: The eighth beatitude returns to the first (kingdom), completing the perfect man; aligned with the sevenfold Spirit (Isa 11); 'for righteousness' guards against suffering for sin.
Persecution not to be sought or provoked; the kingdom belongs only to those who suffer for goodness; the last promise equals the first.
'Righteousness' = virtue, piety, defending others; the kingdom is tacitly meant in every beatitude.
We cannot be Christ's soldiers but the world rises against us; all who live godly suffer persecution (2 Tim 3:12).
Three forms of suffering (reviling, persecution, slander); the blessing requires the evil be false and for God's sake; endure with equanimity (Gregory).
Direct address to disciples; emphasis on 'falsely' (absent from best MSS); reviled like Christ who reviled not again (1 Pet 2:23).
'For my sake' shows Christ's equality with the Father; 'the prophets before you' makes the apostles prophets.
Comfort against being cast out as ungodly by hypocrites; no need to dread even the Pope's excommunication.

sides.position: present and essential (with 'for my sake') to the blessing
absent from the best manuscripts, added as a safeguard

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the call of the fishermen in Mark 1.

schools.summary: 'Be exceeding glad' = leap for joy (martyrs literally did so); the prophets' example comforts; reward great in heaven.
The reward is of grace, not merit (Lk 17:10; Rom 4:4); raising the mind to heaven dispels the sadness of reproach.
Apostles 'salters with eternity' preserving by teaching; if the doctor errs, who corrects him? the savourless are trodden down, i.e. those who fall through fear.
Salt preserves from putrefaction; 'lost its savour' (mōranthē) of insipid salt scattered useless; Dead Sea salt really can lose savour; a likely proverb.
The precepts are for the whole world; like salt the apostles must be biting and reprove, not flatter.
Doctrine is bound to a good conscience; corruption tolerable in others is monstrous in teachers; all the holy, not ministers only (Wesley).
Live well before teaching well; salt to the Jews, light to the Gentiles; the city = the Church / Christ's assumed flesh; bold, not hidden.
Light = the sun's office of making visible; 'light of the world' a rabbinic title transferred to disciples; possibly pointing to Safed on its hill.
The obscure apostles' fame would reach the ends of the earth, persecution making the light more conspicuous.
The title belongs peculiarly to apostles and pastors, set on high on condition that they shine on all (Lk 12:35).
'The bushel' and 'the lamp-stand' (not candle/candlestick) from one-room cottage life; lamp on a wall-stone; hiding it = putting it out.
The lamp is the Gospel, not to be confined or concealed; God's very design is that the light shine.
'Thus let your light shine' — not to be seen of men (Mt 6:1) but that the Father be glorified; we must walk in the light, not only have it.
Reconciles with Mt 6:4: secrecy forbids ostentation, here the object is God's glory alone (2 Cor 8:21).

outliers.note: Eschatological: the righteous shall shine but differ in glory as the stars (Dan 12:3; 1 Cor 15:41).
Stray fragment on the angels' vision of God, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: 'Destroy' = loosen down, 'fulfil' = fill full; the disclaimer forestalls the charge as he ordains greater precepts; aimed at the scribes' traditions, not Moses.
Christ fulfils the prophets by accomplishing prophecy and the Law by keeping it and justifying by faith.
To fulfil = both to do what it commands and to add/perfect; his perfection supplies our infirmity (Augustine).
Of doctrine not life: the gospel agrees perfectly with the Law and brings its complete meaning, against the slander that it ruins the Law.

debates.question: In what sense does Christ 'fulfil' the Law?

sides.position: accomplish its prophecies/types and keep its precepts
add to / perfect and reveal its full depth of meaning

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on Christ's healing tour rather than the Law.

schools.summary: Jot = yod (smallest letter), tittle = a tiny stroke; the Law endures though heaven and earth pass; 'Amen' = fixed, true, used by Christ by divine right (Rev 3:14).
Declines speculation that the last judgment ends the Law; reads it simply that the Law's truth is wholly secure.

outliers.note: Stray catechetical fragment on eternal life by faith, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Against the Pharisees' great/least distinction (Mt 23:23); 'least commandments' = the moral law that seemed trivial; do before teach (Mt 23:3).
The teacher who breaks a command is least, for a despised life makes despised preaching; the iota = ten alludes to the Decalogue.
The 'ten words' are the rule of life; those who weaken the Law's authority are false teachers unworthy of the Church.

debates.question: Do the 'least commandments' include the ceremonial law?

sides.position: no; the moral law only, as NT usage and context show

schools.summary: The scribes' righteousness was external; the Christian's is of the heart; 'exceed' = overflow the banks, out-doing the professional pietists.
'Righteousness' = universal virtue; grace requires untaught disciples to surpass the old masters, confirming the Old Testament.
'Kingdom of heaven' here = the present Church holding both least and great; exceed by doing what the scribes only say (Mt 23:3).
The scribes' zeal for the Law is hypocritical; their boasted righteousness is mere filth.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on baptism of water and Spirit, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: 'Ye have heard' = received by tradition; the citation is the scribes' gloss, not Moses' bare words; 'the judgment' = the local court of twenty-three.
Not a correction or enlargement of the Law but a stripping-away of the Pharisees' crooked expositions to restore its true sense.
Christ vindicates the sixth commandment from corrupt glosses, showing its breadth and spiritual nature.

debates.question: Is Christ correcting Moses or the scribes' tradition?

sides.position: the scribes' tradition/gloss, restoring the Law's true sense

schools.summary: Emphatic 'I say' marks divine authority; Raca (head) and 'fool' (heart) rise from judgment to council to Gehenna; anger at injury is natural, its excess the sin.
Christ's authority overrides antiquity; the degrees teach that even slight movements of anger are condemned by God.
'Without cause' to be erased: anger altogether forbidden; Gehenna from the defiled valley near Jerusalem.

debates.question: Are the words 'without a cause' genuine?

sides.position: spurious; anger itself is forbidden
perhaps original, struck out as giving too wide a margin

schools.summary: Reconciliation before worship: God loves concord more than the gift; 'thy brother hath ought against thee' makes it the more imperative; restore peace with men then with God.
A scene in the Temple: ask not who has wronged me but whom I have wronged; restitution before offering (rabbinic rule extended to all offences).
The duties of religion displease God while we are at variance; no access to God while we keep up a quarrel by our fault.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the count of the Beatitudes rather than this verse.
Applies the verse to the liturgical kiss of peace before the offering.

schools.summary: Leaving the gift unfinished is startling but right; diallagēthi = take the initiative in mutual reconciliation (papyrus parallel); cf. the Passover-leaven rule.
God accepts no worship while enmity remains; gift and prayer become an abomination without love.

outliers.note: Applies the verse to the kiss of peace as the act of reconciliation before the offering.

schools.summary: A plain lesson in legal prudence (Lk 12:58; 1 Cor 6:7): settle debts before trial; the 'officer' is the court attendant; compromise beats prison where only interest is at stake.
Allegorized: the adversary is God's word (not the devil); make peace in this life before death delivers us to the Judge.
Applies to both wronged and wrongdoer; all injuries spring from self-love too tenacious of its rights.

sides.position: a literal legal opponent; counsel of prudence
allegorical (God's word / the time of this life)

schools.summary: The 'farthing' (kodrantes/quadrans) the smallest coin; double negative ou mē paints inevitable punishment for debt.
'Till thou hast paid' = for ever, since the debt can never be paid: an endless penalty.

debates.question: Does the imprisonment imply a terminable or endless punishment?

sides.position: within the parable, terminable on payment (Romanist purgatory inference)
endless, since the debt is unpayable

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the penitent thief and 'today in Paradise,' unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: The scribes limited adultery to the act with a married woman; 'by them of old' absent from best MSS; Christ restores the inward reach.
The command widened to all unchastity; husband to lead in virtue; lust pollutes God's image.
Why this is named 'second' and not the Shema: Christ could not yet bring in himself with the Father.
The law requires holy affections of the heart; Christ blames the false gloss, not Moses.

outliers.note: Stray fragment surveying Israel's history of sin, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: epithumēsai = deliberate coveting, the continued cherished gaze, not a passing glance; the heart = the inner man; 'eye and heart the brokers of sin.'
The habitual gaze surely captures; the alluring woman who 'mixed the poison' is also guilty; avert the eyes at the first (Gregory); propatheia vs passion (Jerome).
By synecdoche, the heart's hidden flames, not the eyes only, make a man an adulterer; chastity is of body and mind (1 Cor 7:34).

outliers.note: Cites the verse to prove Christ's own sinlessness regarding concupiscence.

schools.summary: 'Offend' = skandalizei, ensnare/set a trap; figurative, pleading for self-mastery not mutilation; right eye/hand named as most useful (Mk 9:43).
Allegory: eye = contemplation / cherished friend or counsellor, hand = action; cut off whatever hinders true happiness.
No excuse from weakness: God's justice outweighs all we hold precious; better lose the eye than God's commandments.

sides.position: figurative; self-control of the body against sin

outliers.note: Suggests a transposition: v.29 answers to vv.27-28 (lust), v.30 to vv.21-22 (anger).

schools.summary: The doubled saying is emphatic but distinct: the active gifts/energies, not the senses only, may ensnare; better forfeited than ruinous; the remedy is a will obedient to righteousness.
Gill keeps it to the law of adultery: unchaste touches condemned as the unchaste look was.

outliers.note: Cites the Babylonian Niddah and Rabbi Tarphon on cutting off the wandering, unchaste hand.

schools.summary: Moses permitted divorce for hardness of heart, to check bloodshed, not to sanction it; the bill's difficulty cooled rage (against Manichaeus).
The Hillel-Shammai dispute (any cause vs adultery); 'but it was said' states an implied objection; apostasion = a legal bond of release protecting the wife.
What the national law did not forbid the Jews thought lawful; Moses regulated, not commanded, divorce to restrain lewdness.
Christ tolerates but limits divorce to adultery; the exception clause defended against the claim it was inserted; 'causeth her to commit adultery' implies a divorce a vinculo.
Christ frees the permission from false interpretations, restoring its ancient sense; the unjust divorcer prostitutes his wife to others.

debates.question: Is the 'except for fornication' clause original to Jesus?

sides.position: genuine words of Jesus, rightly reported by Matthew
a later church addition (Mark and Luke omit it)

outliers.note: Records a Jewish objector equating Moses' 'unseemly thing' with fornication, and answers that the adulteress was to be stoned, not merely divorced.

schools.summary: The scribes reduced the third commandment to perjury alone, allowing rash oaths and creature-oaths if the content was true; perform oaths really and religiously.
Safer not to swear at all; the Jews swore by creatures, paying them God's honour; yet Paul calls God to witness.
A true interpretation, not correction: the law condemns lightness in swearing that lessens reverence for God's name (Exod 20:7).
'Swear not at all' = the creature/profane oaths just specified; Jesus answered on oath (Mt 26:63) and Paul swore, so judicial oaths stand; swearing by heaven = by God enthroned (Mt 23:22).
The phrase misleads some to condemn all swearing; context shows the indirect Jewish oaths are meant, added by way of exposition.

debates.question: Does 'swear not at all' forbid all oaths?

sides.position: only rash, profane, and creature-oaths; lawful/judicial oaths remain
all oaths whatever (Society of Friends, with James 5:12)

schools.summary: The Jews swore by the earth and Jerusalem; condemned because the earth is God's footstool and Jerusalem the city of the great King (Ps 48:2) — every such oath reaches back to God.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, commenting on salt and the Beatitudes rather than on oaths.

themes: by Jerusalem / city of the King

schools.summary: Swearing 'by thy head' = by the life, which God authors; man cannot change a hair's colour, so even this oath implies God and is no neutral creature.
Plain truthful speech is the remedy; a man's word as good as his oath (Jas 5:12); profane swearing evidences a depraved heart and wins no extra belief.
Much swearing springs from much falsehood; sincere dealing makes simple speech weigh as much as an oath; the faithful need not swear (Hilary).

debates.question: Is 'cometh of evil' neuter or masculine?

sides.position: evil in the abstract
the evil one (as in the Lord's Prayer)

schools.summary: The lex talionis was enacted to check rage, putting bounds on revenge; the Law corrects faults, the Gospel removes their occasions.
A judicial rule for magistrates, not unjust; a limit in both directions; the Jews wrongly extended it to private revenge; rabbis made it a money payment.
Men seized the magistrate's rule as a pretext for private vengeance; each must bear injuries patiently.
'Evil' = the evil-doer (not the evil one we must resist, Jas 4:7); not to be pressed literally (Jesus protested when smitten, Jn 18:22); forbids repaying outrage with outrage.
Two ways of resisting: warding off injury (allowed) and retaliation (forbidden); overcome evil with good (Rom 12:21); self-defence and lawful redress remain.
Anger is not checked by anger but inflamed; non-resistance keeps the good good; a mystical sense for meeting heretics with Scripture.

debates.question: Is 'resist not evil' the evil man or the evil deed?

sides.position: the human evil-doer
ambiguous; the dative fits either

schools.summary: Coat (chitōn, undergarment) demanded at law, cloke (himation, outer) the more valuable; rather yield than litigate (1 Cor 6:7); the talith bore the gloried-in fringes.
Not to be read woodenly (yielding all before trial would inflame robbers); patient submission over vengeful litigation in purely personal wrongs.
angareuō = forced public courier service (Persian, adopted by Rome); the Latin 'mile'; Simon of Cyrene so compelled (Mt 27:32); willing endurance beyond what is exacted.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the divine authority of 'I say unto you' rather than the verse.

schools.summary: A paradox teaching the giving temper, not indiscriminate almsgiving; tempered by duty to family (1 Tim 5:8) and against idleness (2 Thess 3:10).
Luke 6:30 explains it: disciples generous but not prodigal; liberality governed by the Spirit's rule, with regard to the household of faith.
'Hate thine enemy' is no part of the Law but a rabbinic addition; the Jews narrowed 'neighbour' to fellow-Jew/friend (Lk 10:36).
By the Good Samaritan, the neighbour is everyone needing mercy; loving enemies is not impossible but perfection (David, Stephen, Paul, Christ).
Flesh hates the enemy, spirit loves him; nine ascending steps culminate in love and prayer, the reward likeness to God.
God's 'neighbour' includes the whole human race; in every man I behold myself as in a mirror (Gen 29:14).

debates.question: Is 'hate thine enemy' from the Law?

sides.position: no; a scribal inference Christ repudiates
partly rooted in the commission against Canaan/Amalek, but wrongly stereotyped

outliers.note: Digresses to the souls under the altar crying for vengeance (Abel's blood).

themes: completion of the law of love

schools.summary: Love of benevolence (wishing well) not complacency (approving conduct) owed to enemies; prayer the highest utterance; 'do good to them that hate you' omitted in best MSS.
This sums all the foregoing; obeyable only by self-denial; enemies loved as men though their actions hated (Rom 12:20).
Sonship by adopting grace, shown (not earned) by likeness to God; 'that ye may be like' your Father; striving to be like him in love, not power (Eph 5:1).
An express command whose neglect strikes one from the children of God; imitation limited to God's beneficence, not his punishing.
God sends sun and rain on evil and good alike; 'as' expresses likeness only, not equality (Remigius).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the angels' vision of God, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Reciprocal love is selfish and merits no reward; even the despised publicans (rapacious tax-gatherers) love those who love them.
The tax office is lawful in itself; publicans named for their usual rapacity; the near-inhuman discharge mutual duties to their own interest.
Eastern salutation recognized friends/brothers; the better reading 'heathen' (not publicans); Christians must do more than copy heathen custom.
A glance at sectarian prejudice; would that Christians embraced brethren of every party.

outliers.note: Brief mis-attached note rather than comment on salutation.

schools.summary: teleios = goal/completeness, the Father's absolute standard; an 'ideal future' that is an imperative; a command and a promise (Clarke).
Restrained to love/mercy (Lk 6:36); 'as' = likeness not equality; we are 'perfect' when we aim at the object God presents in himself.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the angels' vision of God, unrelated to the verse.
